name: Superglue Wound Closure
steps: Clean and dry the wound thoroughly. Stop bleeding first.
Push wound edges together precisely.
Apply thin layer of glue ON TOP of the crack, not inside.
Hold shut for 60 seconds until dry.

name: Trapped Digit Amputation
steps: Verify impossibility of rescue. Tourniquet base of finger.
Cut skin and tendons around the bone (circumferential).
Locate the joint (knuckle). Bend finger to open joint gap.
Complete separation. Apply pressure dressing.

name: Frostbite Prevention & Treatment
steps: Recognize: white/gray waxy skin, numbness, hardness.
Rewarm in body heat: armpit, groin, stomach.
If possible, rewarm in 98-102°F water for 20-30 minutes.
Do NOT rewarm if refreezing is possible.

name: Wildfire Survival
steps: Move downhill and into the wind.
Cover mouth with wet cloth. Stay low.
Go to water, roads, cleared areas, or burned zones.
Trapped: clear vegetation, lie face down in depression.

name: Extreme Heat Management
steps: Stop all activity. Find or build shade.
Wet skin/clothes if water available (not drinking water).
Elevate feet. Loosen clothing. Fan yourself.

name: Alligator/Croc Evasion
steps: Stay 15ft from water edge. Don't wash/drink at same spot.
Run in straight line away. Do NOT zigzag.
If bitten: attack eyes and snout. Don't pull.

name: Snake Avoidance
steps: Freeze. Locate the snake.
Identify safe escape route (away from head).
Back away slowly and deliberately.
Stomp feet as you retreat (vibrations warn others).

name: Snake Encounter Protocol
steps: Most snakebites occur when people try to catch or kill snakes.
Wear boots and long pants in snake country.
If bitten: remove rings, watches, and tight clothing near the bite.
Do NOT: cut the bite, suck out venom, apply ice, or apply a tourniquet.
Immobilize the bitten limb below heart level, keep the patient calm, and evacuate.

name: Hypothermia Stage Assessment
steps: MILD (95–90°F / 35–32°C): Shivering, goosebumps, fumbling hands, stumbling.
MODERATE (90–82°F / 32–28°C): Violent shivering or shivering STOPS. Confusion, slurred speech, poor decisions.
SEVERE (<82°F / <28°C): No shivering. Rigid muscles. Unconscious or semi-conscious. Paradoxical undressing.
Handle severe hypothermia patients EXTREMELY gently.
Rewarm the CORE, not the extremities.

name: Heat Exhaustion vs Heat Stroke
steps: HEAT EXHAUSTION: Heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, dizziness, normal or slightly elevated temperature.
Treatment for heat exhaustion: Move to shade, remove excess clothing, drink cool water, apply cool wet cloths.
HEAT STROKE: Sweating STOPS. Hot, dry, red skin. Confusion or unconsciousness. Core temp above 104°F.
Heat stroke treatment: Aggressive emergency cooling.
The transition from heat exhaustion to heat stroke can happen in under 30 minutes.

name: Night Movement Evasion
steps: Move only during the darkest 4 hours (typically 0100-0500).
Use the fox walk: Place the outside edge of your foot down first, roll inward, test for noise.
Navigate by stars or compass with luminous dial.
Stay in shadows and avoid silhouetting against the sky on ridgelines.
If you hear pursuers, freeze completely and remain motionless.

name: Anti-Tracking Techniques
steps: Walk in streams for at least 100 meters before exiting on rocky ground.
Walk backward for 50 meters, then sidestep to a hard surface.
Use the buttonhook: walk past your intended turn, continue 100m, then circle back at a right angle.
Cross hard surfaces (rock, pavement, gravel) whenever possible.
If dogs are tracking, cross through areas with strong-smelling plants (wild onion, camphor).

name: Camouflage Face Paint Application
steps: The human face is highly recognizable due to its symmetry and light-dark contrast.
Darken light areas (forehead, nose, cheekbones, chin) with mud, charcoal, or dark clay.
Lighten dark areas (eye sockets, under nose, neck shadows) with lighter clay or ash.
Use irregular, diagonal stripes — never horizontal or symmetrical patterns.
Cover all exposed skin including ears, backs of hands, and neck.

name: Dog Team Evasion
steps: Move downwind of your trail whenever possible.
Wade through water crossings at least 200 meters long.
Move through areas with strong competing scents: farms, garbage, wild garlic patches.
Pepper spray or chili powder on your trail causes temporary nasal pain and disruption.
The most effective dog evasion is SPEED and DISTANCE.

name: Checkpoint Bypass
steps: Observe the checkpoint from concealment for at least 30 minutes before attempting bypass.
Bypass by routing around it through terrain that is too difficult for guards to watch.
Move during shift changes or high-traffic periods when guards are distracted.
If you must pass through, blend with local pedestrian or vehicle traffic.
Have a cover story with verifiable details prepared.

name: Rockfall Evasion
sourceName: Mountaineering: The Freedom of the Hills
steps: Look UP instantly and identify the fall line.
Dive behind a solid barrier or large tree uphill of you.
If caught without cover, minimize your profile.

name: Sinkhole Escape & Evasion
steps: If the ground gives way, spread your arms wide immediately.
Do NOT stand near the edge. The lip will continue to collapse.
If trapped inside, climb only on stable, embedded roots or solid rock.

name: Flood: Hydrology & Current Evasion
steps: Identify and avoid 'Strainers'.
Target 'Eddies' for refuge.
Escape 'Hydraulic jumps' (Keepers).

name: Flood: Pathogen & HAZMAT Protocol
steps: Assume Class 3 Biohazard status.
Strip and isolate clothing.
Sanitize with extreme dilution.

name: Nuclear Fallout Shelter-in-Place
steps: Get inside the most massive structure available — concrete, brick, underground.
Seal all openings: tape windows, block vents, close all doors.
Stay sheltered for minimum 48 hours, ideally 2 weeks.
If you were outside during the blast: remove ALL clothing, shower or wipe down thoroughly.

name: Snake Encounter Prevention
steps: Watch where you step and put your hands. 80% of bites are on hands and feet.
Wear boots and long pants in snake country.
At night: use a light and clear your sleeping area before lying down.
If you see a snake: freeze, then slowly back away. Most snakes strike within 1/3 of their body length.

name: Heat Exhaustion Emergency Treatment
steps: Recognize: heavy sweating, weakness, cold/clammy skin, nausea, fast weak pulse.
Move to shade or coolest available area immediately.
Remove excess clothing. Apply cool water to skin, fan the patient.
If patient stops sweating, becomes confused, or has hot dry skin: this is HEAT STROKE. Emergency.

name: Snow Blindness Prevention
steps: Recognize the massive UV threat.
Do not squint; it is not enough.
Carve Inuit-style slit goggles.
Charcoal the ocular cavities.

name: Anti-Tracking & Evasion
steps: Walk on hard surfaces: rock, gravel, packed earth. Avoid mud and soft ground.
Brush out visible tracks behind you with a leafy branch.
Change direction at hard-surface intersections to break the trail.
Avoid breaking branches, disturbing spider webs, or crushing vegetation.

name: Rib Fracture Management
steps: Confirm rib fracture: point tenderness on ribs, pain worse with breathing/coughing.
DO NOT wrap or bind the chest tightly.
Practice deep breathing exercises every hour despite the pain.
Position for sleep: sleep on the INJURED side.
Monitor for pneumothorax: sudden worsening, one-sided chest expansion.

name: Choking Response (Complete Guide)
steps: Assess: can they cough, speak, or breathe? If yes — encourage coughing.
Give 5 sharp back blows between the shoulder blades with heel of your hand.
If back blows fail: 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver).
Alternate: 5 back blows, then 5 abdominal thrusts until cleared.
For infants: 5 back blows with baby face-down on your forearm, then 5 chest thrusts.

name: Log Cabin Fire Lay
steps: Lay 2 parallel sticks on the ground, 1 foot apart.
Stack 2 more sticks perpendicular on top, forming a square.
Continue stacking 3-4 layers high. Place tinder in the center hollow.
Light the center tinder. The structure burns inward and collapses into coals.

name: Battery & Steel Wool Fire
steps: Stretch steel wool into a thin, loose pad.
Touch both terminals of a 9V battery to the steel wool.
The steel wool will glow and burn rapidly.
Transfer to tinder bundle immediately.

name: Potassium Permanganate Fire
steps: Place a small pile of potassium permanganate on dry surface.
Add a few drops of glycerin (antifreeze works too).
Stand back—the mixture will spontaneously ignite.

name: Carrying Fire / Ember Transport
steps: Place hot coal in bundle of dry punk wood or fungus.
Wrap in green leaves to insulate and contain.
Blow gently to feed ember when needed.
At new camp: place in tinder bundle, blow to flame.

name: Pine Resin Torch
steps: Collect pine resin from wounds on pine or spruce trees.
Wrap resin-soaked cloth or bark around the end of a green stick.
Light the torch. Burns 20-40 minutes with bright flame.

name: Star Fire Configuration
steps: Arrange 4-6 large logs in a star pattern with ends meeting at center.
Light a small fire at the center where the logs meet.
Push logs inward as they burn. No chopping required.

name: Vaseline Cotton Ball Fire Starter
steps: Work petroleum jelly thoroughly into a cotton ball.
Pull the cotton ball apart slightly to expose dry fibers.
Ignite the dry fibers. The flame will spread to the petroleum-soaked portion.

name: Upside-Down Fire (Self-Feeding)
steps: Lay largest logs on the bottom in a tight row.
Stack progressively smaller wood in layers, perpendicular to the layer below.
Light the tinder on top. Fire burns downward through layers.

name: Char Rope Making
steps: Find natural fiber rope: cotton, jute, hemp, or sisal.
Place rope in a metal container with a small vent hole. Cook in fire.
Test: char rope should catch a spark and glow for extended time.

name: Fire Safety Perimeter
steps: Clear all flammable material in a 10-foot circle around the fire pit.
Build a ring of rocks around the fire pit.
Keep water or loose soil staged nearby for emergency extinguishing.

name: Coal-Burned Container
steps: Select a log section of appropriate size — green or slightly dry hardwood works best.
Place a hot coal from your fire on the surface where you want to hollow.
Blow gently on the coal to maintain heat. Add new coals as needed.
Scrape away charred wood with a sharp stone or shell. Repeat: burn, scrape, burn, scrape.
Control the burn area with wet clay or mud paste around the edges of the desired shape.

name: Fire Management in Rain
steps: Build your fire on a raised platform of green logs if ground is saturated.
Create a rain cover: lean a bark slab or tarp at an angle over the fire, high enough to avoid catching fire.
Maintain a larger fire than usual — more mass resists rain extinguishment.
Store dry firewood NEAR the fire (not on it) to keep it dry. Under a tarp or sheltered overhang.
If fire dies to coals, protect the coal bed from rain and rebuild with feather sticks and dry tinder.

name: Pine Pitch Torch
steps: Find a fatwood source — the resin-saturated heartwood of dead pine stumps.
Split fatwood into a bundle of sticks about 12 inches long and finger-thickness.
Alternatively: wrap cloth around a green stick and soak with melted pine resin.
Light the torch — fatwood ignites from a match, ferro rod, or existing fire. Burns with a bright, smoky flame.
To extinguish: press the burning end into dirt or sand. Do NOT stomp — this scatters burning material.

name: Steel Wool & Battery Fire
steps: Pull apart fine-grade steel wool (0000 grade) into a loose, airy ball about fist-sized.
Use a 9V battery (ideal) or any battery 3V+. Touch both terminals to the steel wool simultaneously.
The steel wool glows orange and burns quickly. Have your tinder bundle ready beneath.
No steel wool? Any fine metal strand works: picture wire, wire brush strands, metal scrubbing pads.
This method works in rain and wind — the chemical reaction doesn't require the spark to catch. Just contact.

name: Permanganate & Sugar
steps: Potassium Permanganate (purple water purification crystals) produces severe exothermic reactions when mixed with certain organic compounds or sugars.
Place one teaspoon of Potassium Permanganate crystals onto a flat, dry rock.
Add an equal amount of granulated sugar to the pile of purple crystals.
Grind the two powders together vigorously using a smooth, blunt stick.
Step back instantly as soon as it begins to smoke.

name: Brake Fluid & Pool Shock
steps: WARNING: This combination produces violently toxic chlorine gas before combusting.
Pool shock relies on Calcium Hypochlorite (a massive oxidizer). Brake fluid relies on Polyethylene Glycol.
Pour 2 tablespoons of Calcium Hypochlorite onto a dry piece of cardboard or wood.
Pour 1 tablespoon of DOT-3 or DOT-4 brake fluid directly onto the powder.
Run immediately upwind.

name: Banking a Fire for Overnight Burn
steps: Build a thick coal bed
Cover coals with ash
Place a large green log on top
Uncover and add tinder in the morning

name: Fire from Batteries (Any Type)
steps: Find a battery and any thin conductive material: steel wool, foil gum wrapper, or wire.
Steel wool method: touch both battery terminals to fine steel wool.
Gum wrapper method: cut the foil-backed wrapper into an hourglass shape (thin center).
Car battery: touch jumper cable ends together briefly near tinder.

name: Burn Blister Management
steps: Cool the burn under cool running water for 10-20 minutes.
Do NOT pop burn blisters — the fluid protects healing skin.
Cover loosely with non-stick gauze or clean cloth.
Elevate the burned area above heart level if possible.
Watch for infection: increasing redness, pus, fever, red streaks.

name: Severe Burn (Third Degree)
steps: Call emergency services immediately. This is life-threatening.
Do NOT remove burnt clothing stuck to the skin.
Cover loosely with clean, dry cloth. Do NOT apply water.
Elevate burned area above heart level.
Treat for shock: lay flat, keep warm, monitor breathing.

name: Head Trauma Assessment
steps: Keep the person still. Do NOT move them unless in immediate danger.
Check consciousness: ask their name, where they are, what happened.
Control any bleeding with gentle direct pressure.
Check for clear fluid leaking from ears or nose.
Monitor every 15 minutes: pupils, consciousness, breathing.

name: Concussion Management
steps: Remove person from activity. Physical and mental rest is critical.
Apply cold pack to the impact area for 20 minutes.
Monitor for worsening symptoms over 24-48 hours.
Wake the person every 2-3 hours for the first night.
No blood-thinning painkillers (aspirin/ibuprofen) for 24 hours.

name: Concussion Quick Guide
steps: Stop all activity. Sit them down in a safe location.
Check pupils: shine light in each eye. Both should be equal size.
Test orientation: name, location, day of the week.
Check balance: stand with eyes closed for 30 seconds.
Monitor for 24 hours. Wake every 2 hours during sleep.

name: Choking Response (Heimlich)
steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' — if they can't speak or cough, act immediately.
Stand behind the person, wrap arms around their waist.
Make a fist, place thumb side against abdomen above navel.
Grasp fist with other hand. Thrust inward and upward sharply.
If object doesn't dislodge: alternate 5 back blows with 5 thrusts.

name: Emergency Tourniquet
steps: Place tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound.
Pull strap tight and secure it.
Twist the windlass rod until bleeding stops.
Lock the windlass in place and note the time.

name: Pressure Bandage
steps: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth.
Elevate the wound above the heart if possible.
Wrap with elastic bandage snugly but not cutting off circulation.
If blood soaks through, add more material on top.

name: CPR (Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation)
steps: Check responsiveness. Tap shoulders and shout.
Place heel of hand on center of chest, between nipples.
Compress chest 2 inches deep at 100-120 beats per minute.
Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions.
Continue 30:2 cycle without stopping until help arrives.

name: Heimlich Maneuver (Choking)
steps: Confirm choking — person cannot speak, cough, or breathe.
Stand behind the person and wrap arms around their waist.
Make a fist with one hand, place thumb side against abdomen above navel.
Thrust inward and upward with quick, forceful movements.

name: Treating Shock
steps: Recognize shock: pale/cool/clammy skin, rapid weak pulse, confusion.
Lay the person flat and elevate legs 8-12 inches.
Maintain body temperature — cover with blankets or coats.
Do NOT give food or water. Moisten lips only.

name: Recovery Position
steps: Kneel beside the person. Extend near arm at right angle.
Place far hand against their near cheek, hold it there.
Pull the far knee up with your other hand.
Roll them toward you by pulling the raised knee.

name: Arm/Wrist Splint
steps: Do NOT attempt to realign the bone.
Find a rigid splint material: stick, cardboard, rolled magazine.
Pad the splint with cloth and secure with bandages or strips.
Check circulation below the splint: warmth, color, feeling, pulse.

name: Leg Splint
steps: Immobilize the leg in the position found.
Use two long rigid objects as splints — one inside, one outside the leg.
Pad between the leg and splints generously.
Secure with wide bandages at 4-5 points. Avoid tying over the break.

name: Arm Sling
steps: Fold a square cloth into a triangle.
Slide the triangle under the injured arm and behind the elbow.
Tie the two upper points behind the neck.
Pin or tie the elbow point to secure it.

name: Burn Treatment
steps: Remove from heat source. Remove clothing/jewelry from burn area.
Cool the burn with cool (not cold) running water for 20 minutes.
Cover with cling wrap or a clean non-fluffy dressing.
Do NOT pop blisters. They are sterile bandages.

name: Chemical Burn
steps: Brush off dry chemicals before adding water.
Flush with large amounts of water for at least 20 minutes.
Remove contaminated clothing while flushing.
Do NOT try to neutralize the chemical.

name: Snakebite Management
steps: Move away from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it.
Keep the bitten limb below heart level and immobilize it.
Do NOT cut, suck, tourniquet, or apply ice to the bite.
Mark the edge of swelling and note the time.
Evacuate to a hospital. Antivenom is the only treatment.

name: Bee/Wasp Sting Treatment
steps: Scrape the stinger out sideways with a flat edge.
Clean with soap and water. Apply cold pack for 15 minutes.
Watch for anaphylaxis: facial swelling, difficulty breathing, hives.
For mild reactions: antihistamine and pain reliever.

name: Anaphylaxis Response
steps: Recognize: throat tightening, facial swelling, hives, wheezing, dizziness.
Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into outer thigh.
Call emergency services immediately.
Place in position of comfort. Usually sitting up for breathing.

name: Hypothermia Treatment
steps: Get the person out of the cold and remove wet clothing.
Insulate from the ground and cover with multiple layers.
Apply warm (not hot) packs to core areas: neck, armpits, groin.
Give warm sweet drinks if conscious and alert.
Handle gently — a severely hypothermic heart is fragile.

name: Heat Stroke Treatment
steps: Recognize: hot/red/dry skin, confusion, temp over 104°F, loss of consciousness.
Move to shade. Remove excess clothing.
Cool aggressively: wet the entire body and fan.
Evacuate immediately. Call emergency services.

name: Dental Emergency
steps: Pick up the tooth by the crown (white part), NEVER the root.
Rinse gently with milk or saliva. Do NOT scrub.
Attempt to reinsert into the socket.
Get to a dentist within 30 minutes.

name: Seizure Management
steps: Clear area around the person of hard or sharp objects.
Place something soft under their head.
Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
Time the seizure. Over 5 minutes is a medical emergency.

name: Impaled Object Management
steps: Do NOT remove the impaled object.
Stabilize the object so it cannot move.
Control bleeding around the object with direct pressure.
Immobilize the body part and transport carefully.

name: Heart Attack Recognition
steps: Recognize: crushing chest pressure, left arm/jaw pain, shortness of breath, cold sweat.
Sit person upright, knees bent, back supported.
Give aspirin if available — have them chew it.
If unresponsive, begin CPR immediately.

name: Stroke Recognition (FAST)
steps: FACE: Ask them to smile. Does one side droop?
ARMS: Raise both arms. Does one drift down?
SPEECH: Repeat a sentence. Is it slurred?
TIME: Note when symptoms started. Get help NOW.

name: Infant Choking Response
steps: Place infant face-down on forearm, head lower than chest.
5 firm back blows between shoulder blades.
Flip face-up. 5 chest thrusts with two fingers on breastbone.
Repeat until expelled. If unconscious, begin infant CPR.

name: Dislocated Joint Management
steps: Do NOT try to pop the joint back in.
Immobilize in current position with splint or sling.
Apply cold compress. 20 minutes on, 20 off.
Check circulation below injury: pulse, sensation, color.

name: Wound Packing
steps: Identify the wound as deep and bleeding heavily despite direct pressure.
Using gauze or clean cloth, push material INTO the wound cavity.
Apply firm direct pressure on top of the packed wound for 3 minutes.
Secure with a pressure bandage. Monitor for continued bleeding.

name: Eye Injury Response
steps: Do NOT rub, press, or touch the injured eye.
For foreign objects: flush with clean water. Do NOT remove embedded objects.
Shield the injured eye with a rigid cover (cup, cardboard cone).
Cover BOTH eyes to prevent sympathetic movement.

name: Spinal Immobilization
steps: Suspect spinal injury after any fall, dive, vehicle accident, or head impact.
Tell the patient: DO NOT MOVE. Stabilize the head and neck manually.
Improvise a cervical collar from rolled clothing or padding.
Do NOT move the patient unless there is immediate danger.

name: Improvised Chest Seal
steps: Identify the wound: look for bubbling blood or sucking sound on chest.
Expose the wound completely — remove clothing around it.
Cover wound with plastic (bag, wrapper, card) taped on THREE sides.
Position person sitting upright, leaning toward injured side.
Monitor breathing closely. If breathing worsens, briefly lift seal.

name: Heat Stroke Emergency Treatment
steps: Move person to shade or coolest available area immediately.
Remove excess clothing. Expose skin to air.
Cool aggressively: pour water on skin, fan vigorously.
If conscious, give small sips of cool water.
Monitor mental status: confusion, seizures, unconsciousness.

name: Hypothermia Field Treatment
steps: Get person out of cold environment and wind.
Remove ALL wet clothing. Replace with dry layers.
Insulate from the ground. Place padding underneath.
Apply heat to core: neck, armpits, groin.
Give warm sweet drinks if conscious and alert.

name: Severe Allergic Reaction Response
steps: Identify signs: hives, swelling, difficulty breathing, dizziness.
If EpiPen available: inject into outer thigh, hold 10 seconds.
Have person sit upright if breathing is difficult.
Remove the allergen source if possible.
Monitor for at least 4 hours — reactions can return.

name: Choking Response (Adult & Child)
steps: Determine severity: can they cough, speak, or breathe?
Stand behind, give 5 sharp back blows between shoulder blades.
If back blows fail: give 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich).
Alternate: 5 back blows, then 5 abdominal thrusts. Repeat.
If person becomes unconscious: lower to ground, begin CPR.

name: Junctional Hemorrhage Control
steps: Apply direct pressure with hemostatic gauze if available.
For junctional wounds (neck, groin, armpit): pack tightly and hold.
Maintain constant, firm pressure for minimum 3 minutes.
Apply pressure bandage over the packed wound.
Elevate the wound above heart level if possible.

name: Poisoning Emergency Response
steps: Identify the poison: what, how much, when, and how.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically told to by poison control.
If poison is on skin: remove contaminated clothing, rinse with water.
If poison inhaled: move to fresh air immediately.
Monitor for shock, breathing difficulty, or seizures.

name: Frostbite Treatment
steps: Move to warm shelter. Do NOT rub or massage affected area.
Remove wet clothing and constricting items from affected area.
Rewarm in warm (not hot) water: 98-102°F (37-39°C) for 30 minutes.
Do NOT rewarm if refreezing is possible.
Protect rewarmed tissue: wrap loosely, separate fingers/toes.

name: Snake Bite Response
steps: Move AWAY from the snake. Do NOT try to catch or kill it.
Keep the bitten limb BELOW heart level and still.
Do NOT: cut wound, suck venom, apply ice, or use tourniquet.
Clean the bite gently with water. Mark the edge of swelling with pen.
Keep calm and still. Monitor for systemic symptoms.

name: Deep Splinter & Foreign Body Removal
steps: Clean the area with soap and water before removal.
Sterilize tweezers or needle with flame or alcohol.
Grasp the splinter near the skin and pull along the angle of entry.
Squeeze the wound gently to encourage bleeding.
Clean again, apply antibiotic if available, and cover.

name: Knee Injury Stabilization
steps: Immobilize the knee in the position found.
Check circulation below the knee: pulse, warmth, sensation in foot.
Apply cold to reduce swelling for 20 minutes.
Wrap with compression bandage in figure-8 pattern.
Create a walking aid: trekking pole, sturdy branch, or crutch.

name: Femur Fracture Traction Splint
steps: Suspect femur fracture: severe thigh pain, inability to move leg, leg appears shortened.
Check for circulation, sensation, and movement in foot before splinting.
Create traction splint: one long pole from armpit to past foot.
Secure splint at chest, hip, above knee, and at ankle.
Apply gentle traction at ankle to realign and reduce pain.

name: Flail Chest Management
steps: Recognize flail chest: a section of ribs moves OPPOSITE to breathing (inward on inhale, outward on exhale).
Have the patient lie on the injured side, or stabilize the flail segment with your hand.
Do NOT wrap the entire chest — this restricts breathing of the good lung.
Monitor oxygen level: watch for blue lips, confusion, rapid heart rate, worsening distress.
Evacuate urgently. Keep patient semi-upright (45°) for easiest breathing.

name: Pelvic Fracture Management
steps: Suspect pelvic fracture if: pain in groin/hip area, inability to bear weight, leg appears shortened or rotated.
Do NOT move the patient unless in immediate danger. Stabilize where they lie.
Wrap a sheet, belt, or SAM splint tightly around the hips at the level of the greater trochanters.
Place a rolled blanket or pack between the knees and tie the legs together.
Treat for shock: keep warm, elevate legs only if no spinal injury suspected, monitor consciousness.

name: Basic Splinting Principles
steps: Splint in the position found. Do NOT try to straighten a deformed limb.
Splint must immobilize the joint ABOVE and BELOW the fracture.
Pad the splint generously — especially at bony prominences.
Tie splint firmly but not so tight that circulation is cut off. Check pulse, sensation, and movement below.
Materials: sticks, trekking poles, rolled magazines, SAM splints, cardboard, bark slabs, foam pads.

name: Heat Illness Spectrum
steps: Heat Cramps (mild): muscle spasms during exertion. Stop activity, rest in shade, drink electrolyte water.
Heat Exhaustion (moderate): heavy sweating, weakness, nausea, headache, cool clammy skin, core temp under 104°F.
Heat Stroke (CRITICAL): core temp above 104°F, confusion, hot DRY skin, seizures, unconsciousness.
For heat stroke: cool AGGRESSIVELY. Immerse in cold water, pack ice around neck, armpits, groin.
Do NOT give fluids to a heat stroke patient who is confused or unconscious. Continue cooling until temp drops to 101°F.

name: Traumatic Amputation Response
steps: Control bleeding on the stump FIRST. Apply direct pressure, then tourniquet if bleeding is severe.
Wrap the stump with sterile gauze or clean cloth. Apply firm pressure bandage.
Treat for shock: lay flat, elevate legs, keep warm, reassure.
Recover the severed part if safe to do so. Rinse gently with clean water.
Wrap the part in moist gauze, place in a sealed bag, then place bag on ice. Do NOT freeze or submerge in water.

name: Emergency Eye Irrigation
steps: Chemical exposure: begin flushing IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait to identify the chemical.
Use clean water — pour steadily from a bottle or cup. Hold eye open with fingers.
Flush continuously for minimum 15-20 minutes for chemicals. 5 minutes for debris.
For embedded debris (metal, glass): do NOT rub. Cover with a cup or cone taped over the eye.
After flushing: cover loosely with clean cloth. Do not patch tightly. Seek medical care.

name: Seizure Response
steps: Do NOT restrain the person. Do NOT put anything in their mouth.
Clear the area of hazards: move furniture, sharp objects, rocks away from the person.
Time the seizure. If it exceeds 5 minutes, this is a medical emergency.
Once convulsions stop, roll the person onto their side (recovery position).
Stay with them until fully alert. Speak calmly. Orient them: 'You had a seizure. You are safe. It's [time].'

name: Hypothermia Wrap
steps: Remove all wet clothing. Cut off if patient is shivering too hard to undress.
Lay a vapor barrier (tarp, emergency blanket, trash bags) on the ground first.
Wrap patient in dry insulation layers: sleeping bag, blankets, dry clothing. Cover head.
Add heat sources to the core: warm water bottles at neck, armpits, and groin.
Wrap the outer layer with another vapor barrier (tarp/emergency blanket). Create a 'burrito.'

name: Femoral Artery Pressure Point
steps: Locate the femoral artery: in the crease where the leg meets the groin, midway between the hip bone and pubic bone.
Press firmly with the heel of your hand directly over the pulse point.
Maintain constant pressure while another person applies direct pressure or a tourniquet to the wound.
If alone: apply a tourniquet high and tight on the upper thigh, then release pressure point.
Monitor for shock: keep the patient warm, elevate uninjured legs, and reassure them.

name: Emergency Dental Pain Relief
steps: For a lost filling: pack the cavity with softened candle wax, chewing gum, or pine resin.
For toothache: apply crushed clove (eugenol) or a piece of fresh garlic directly to the painful tooth.
For a knocked-out tooth: keep it moist in saliva (between cheek and gum) or milk. Reimplant within 30 minutes if possible.
For a broken tooth: cover sharp edges with wax to prevent tongue/cheek cuts. Take pain relief if available.
For an abscess (swelling with pus): rinse with warm salt water every 2 hours. Do NOT pop. Seek evacuation.

name: Core Temperature Assessment
steps: Touch the skin at the abdomen or armpit — these reflect core temperature better than extremities.
Shivering assessment: mild hypothermia = vigorous shivering. Severe = shivering STOPS (very dangerous sign).
Mental status: mild cold = clear thinking. Moderate = confusion, slurred speech. Severe = unconscious.
For heat: hot, DRY skin = heat stroke (emergency). Hot, WET skin = heat exhaustion (serious but less critical).
Pulse assessment: cold = slow, weak pulse. Heat stroke = rapid, bounding pulse. Both indicate core temp emergency.

name: Cravat Bandage Sling
steps: Fold a square cloth (shirt, bandana, etc.) diagonally to create a triangle.
Place the forearm across the triangle with the elbow at the point and the hand toward the longest edge.
Bring both ends up and tie behind the neck. Adjust so the forearm is supported across the chest.
Pin or tie the elbow point to prevent the arm from swinging.
Check circulation: fingers should stay pink, warm, and mobile. Adjust tightness if circulation is compromised.

name: Field Wound Closure
steps: ONLY close wounds that are clean, less than 6 hours old, and have smooth edges.
Butterfly strips: cut tape into narrow strips with wider ends. Apply perpendicular to the wound, pulling edges together.
Improvised sutures: use a sterilized fishhook or needle and clean thread (dental floss, inner paracord strand).
Simple interrupted suture: enter 1/4 inch from wound edge, pass through both sides, tie with square knot.
Monitor for infection daily: redness spreading from wound, increased swelling, warmth, pus, or red streaks = remove sutures immediately.

name: Hands-Only CPR
steps: Confirm the person is unresponsive: tap shoulders firmly and shout 'Are you okay?'
Call for help. If alone, call emergency services first if you have a phone. Speaker mode.
Place the heel of one hand on the center of the chest (between the nipples). Stack the other hand on top.
Push HARD and FAST: compress at least 2 inches deep, at 100-120 beats per minute.
DO NOT STOP until: the person starts breathing, professional help arrives, or you are physically unable to continue.

name: Chemical Water Purification
steps: Iodine: add 5-10 drops of 2% tincture of iodine per liter of clear water. Double for cloudy water.
Bleach: add 2 drops of unscented household bleach (5-8% sodium hypochlorite) per liter of clear water.
Wait 30 minutes before drinking. If water is cold or cloudy, wait 60 minutes.
After treatment, water should have a slight chemical taste/smell. If not, add another dose and wait 15 more minutes.
Limitations: chemical treatment does NOT remove chemical contaminants, and may not kill Cryptosporidium.

name: Snake Bite Pressure Immobilization
steps: DO NOT: cut the wound, suck venom, apply ice, use a tourniquet, or apply electric shock.
Keep the victim CALM and STILL. Movement accelerates venom spread through the lymphatic system.
For neurotoxic snakes (cobras, mambas, sea snakes): apply a compression bandage starting at the bite, wrapping toward the heart.
Immobilize the bitten limb with a splint. Movement of muscles pumps lymph fluid, spreading venom.
For hemotoxic snakes (vipers, rattlesnakes): do NOT apply compression bandage. Keep limb below heart level and evacuate.

name: Dislocated Shoulder Reduction
steps: Confirm dislocation: the shoulder looks squared off (not rounded), arm is held away from body, intense pain with movement.
Cunningham technique (safest): have the patient sit upright. Gently massage the muscles around the shoulder.
While massaging, instruct the patient to slowly shrug their shoulders upward and squeeze their shoulder blades together.
If the Cunningham technique fails: lay patient face-down with the injured arm hanging over a ledge. Hang a weight (8-10 lbs) from the wrist.
After reduction: immobilize in a sling. Ice if available. Do NOT use the arm for overhead activities.

name: Active Rewarming Techniques
steps: Insulate from the ground FIRST — ground conducts heat 25x faster than air.
Warm the core, NOT the extremities. Apply heat to the neck, armpits, and groin.
Heat sources: hot water bottles (plastic bottles filled with warm water), heated rocks wrapped in cloth, body-to-body contact.
Warm, sweet drinks if the patient is conscious and can swallow. NO alcohol.
Rewarming takes HOURS, not minutes. Monitor continuously. Severe hypothermia patients may appear dead but still be viable.

name: Field Spinal Assessment
steps: Mechanism of injury: fall from height, vehicle accident, diving, or force to head/neck = suspect spine injury.
Ask: 'Do you have any neck or back pain?' If yes — immobilize. Do not move the patient.
Test sensation: can they feel you touch their fingers and toes? Compare both sides.
Test motor: can they wiggle fingers and toes on command? Can they squeeze your hands equally?
If alert, no pain, full sensation, full motor, and no intoxication — spine can be cautiously cleared for movement.

name: improvised Hemostatic Agents
steps: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium): crush fresh leaves and pack directly into the wound.
Spider web: collect and pack clean spider webs into minor wounds.
Wood ash (clean, white ash from hardwood): pack into wounds as a last resort.
Coffee grounds: unused, dry grounds packed into wounds promote clotting.
These are supplements to direct pressure — they do NOT replace it. Maintain firm pressure over any natural agent applied.

name: Improvised Rigid Foam Splint
steps: Cut a section of a closed-cell foam sleeping pad long enough to extend past the joints above and below the fracture.
Fold the pad lengthways into a U-shape or 'gutter'.
Pad the injured limb with spare clothing before applying the foam gutter.
Place the limb in the gutter and secure it tightly with cravats, ace bandages, or duct tape.
Elevate the splinted limb.

name: Improvised Tourniquet
steps: A tourniquet is ONLY for life-threatening arterial bleeding that cannot be controlled by direct pressure.
Use a 2-inch-wide strip of material (belt, shirt strip, bandana). Never use narrow cords or wire.
Place the tourniquet 2 to 3 inches above the wound (between the wound and the heart).
Tie a half-knot, place a sturdy stick on top, tie a full knot over the stick.
Secure the windlass and write the time of application on the patient's forehead.

name: Shoulder Reduction (Field)
steps: Confirm anterior shoulder dislocation: the arm is held away from the body, the shoulder looks squared off, and the patient cannot rotate the arm inward.
Have the patient sit upright in a chair, leaning slightly forward.
Have the patient shrug their shoulders upward and backward, pinching the shoulder blades together.
Gently massage the bicep, deltoid, and trapezius muscles to release the spasm.
Once reduced, immobilize the arm in a sling against the body.

name: Field Dental Emergency
steps: Knocked-out tooth: Pick it up by the CROWN (the white part), never the root.
Attempt to reimplant the tooth into the socket immediately.
If you cannot reimplant it, store the tooth in milk, saliva, or held inside the cheek.
Lost filling or cracked tooth: Pack the cavity with sugar-free gum, dental wax, or candle wax.
Tooth abscess: Rinse with warm salt water. Take anti-inflammatory medication.

name: Choking (Heimlich Maneuver)
steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' If they cannot speak, cough, or breathe, they have a complete obstruction.
Stand behind the victim. Wrap your arms around their waist.
Grasp your fist with the other hand. Perform firm, quick, inward-and-upward thrusts.
Repeat until the object is expelled or the victim becomes unconscious.
For infants: Use back blows and chest thrusts, NOT abdominal thrusts.

name: Butterfly Closure Strips
steps: Clean the wound thoroughly with clean water under pressure.
Dry the skin on BOTH sides of the wound completely.
Apply tincture of benzoin to the skin on both sides of the wound.
Apply butterfly strips perpendicular to the wound, pulling the edges together firmly.
Cover with a sterile gauze pad and tape.

name: Frostbite Rewarming Protocol
steps: Do NOT rewarm frostbitten tissue if there is ANY chance it will refreeze.
Immerse the affected area in water heated to 98-102°F (37-39°C).
Keep the water temperature constant for 30-60 minutes.
EXTREME PAIN accompanies successful rewarming.
After rewarming, place sterile gauze between fingers/toes, wrap loosely, and elevate.

name: Shock Treatment Protocol
steps: Recognize shock: pale/ashen skin, rapid weak pulse, rapid shallow breathing, confusion, anxiety.
Control the cause: stop bleeding, splint fractures, treat allergic reactions.
Lay the patient flat and elevate legs 12 inches.
Prevent heat loss aggressively.
Give small sips of water if the patient is conscious and not vomiting.

name: Spinal Log Roll
steps: The log roll requires 3 people minimum: head holder + 2 body controllers.
The head holder is in charge.
Body controllers kneel beside the patient: one at shoulders/hips, one at hips/legs.
On command, roll the patient toward the controllers in ONE smooth motion.
Slide the backboard or pad under the patient, then roll them back.

name: Improvised Cervical Collar
steps: A cervical collar prevents flexion, extension, and rotation of the neck.
Use an SAM splint, rolled newspaper, or a folded towel wrapped inside a triangular bandage.
The collar must be tall enough to prevent the patient from looking down.
It must NOT compress the trachea or jugular veins.
A collar alone does NOT clear the spine.

name: Activated Charcoal for Poisoning
steps: Activated charcoal adsorbs many poisons and prevents them from entering the bloodstream.
Grind hardwood charcoal into the finest possible powder.
Mix 2-3 tablespoons of charcoal powder into a cup of water.
Most effective within 1 hour of ingestion.
Charcoal does NOT work against: alcohols, cyanide, heavy metals, or caustic acids/bases.

name: Advanced Airway: Jaw Thrust
steps: Kneel at the victim's head.
Place palms on cheekbones.
Hook fingers under the jaw angle.
Lift the jaw upward.

name: Manage Tension Pneumothorax
steps: Monitor for worsening breathing.
Locate the chest seal edge.
Lift one edge upon exhalation.
Reseal the dressing.

name: Improvised Pelvic Binder
steps: Identify pelvic fracture.
Prepare a broad sheet.
Slide under the greater trochanters.
Pull tight and cross over.
Tie off or secure.

name: Hemostatic Wound Packing
steps: Identify source of massive bleed.
Push gauze deep into the cavity.
Pack tightly until full.
Apply direct downward pressure.

name: Severe Hypothermia Wrap
steps: Remove wet clothing.
Layer a tarp and sleeping pad.
Add sleeping bags and patient.
Wrap the outer tarp.

name: Frostbite Rapid Rewarming
steps: Ensure no risk of refreezing.
Prepare a warm water bath.
Immerse the frozen tissue.
Circulate and refill.
Air dry and pad.

name: Heat Stroke Rapid Cooling
steps: Recognize Heat Stroke.
Create a tarp basin.
Add ice and water.
Agitate the water.
Cool then transport.

name: Dry Chemical Decontamination
steps: Do NOT use water immediately.
Brush off the powder.
Remove contaminated clothing.
Flush ONLY after brushing.

name: Wet Chemical Decontamination
steps: Protect yourself first.
Remove saturated clothing.
Irrigate with copious water.
Direct water away.

name: Tear Gas/OC Recovery
steps: Move to fresh air.
Do NOT rub eyes.
Blink rapidly and flush.
Blow nose, do not swallow.

name: Improvised Pelvic Binder (Sheet)
steps: Fold sheet lengthwise
Slide under patient at hip level
Cross ends over pelvis and pull tight
Tie or clamp in place

name: Morgan Lens Eye Irrigation
steps: Flush immediately with clean water
Pull lower lid down
Irrigate for minimum 20 minutes
Check pH if possible

name: Improvised Traction Splint
steps: Pad the ankle and groin
Secure two rigid poles alongside the leg
Apply traction via ankle hitch
Twist a windlass to apply steady pull

name: Field Core Temperature Check
steps: Understand why oral temp fails
Use rectal or esophageal probe if available
Stage hypothermia by core temp
Document and monitor trends

name: Circumferential Burn Assessment
steps: Check for circumferential burns
Assess distal circulation
Elevate burned extremities
Prepare for escharotomy

name: Knocked-Out Tooth Preservation
steps: Handle tooth by crown only
Reimplant within 30 minutes if possible
If can't reimplant, store properly
Seek dental care within 2 hours

name: Impaled Object Stabilization
steps: NEVER remove impaled objects
Stabilize with bulky dressings
Tape or bandage to prevent movement
Shorten if necessary for transport

name: Open Fracture Field Management
steps: Control bleeding first
Irrigate wound with clean water
Cover with moist sterile dressing
Splint in position found

name: Pit Viper Snakebite (Rattlesnake, Copperhead, Cottonmouth)
steps: Move away from the snake. Note appearance but do NOT try to catch it.
Remove jewelry and constricting clothing near the bite.
IMMOBILIZE the bitten limb at or below heart level.
Evacuate to hospital. Antivenom is the only definitive treatment.

name: Coral Snake Bite
steps: Identify: red-yellow-black bands. 'Red touches yellow, kills a fellow.'
Apply a pressure immobilization bandage: firm wrap starting at the bite and wrapping toward the heart.
IMMOBILIZE entirely — splint the limb.
Transport immediately. Symptoms may be delayed 12+ hours but can progress to respiratory failure.

name: Pediatric Choking (Infant < 1 year)
steps: Position infant face-down along your forearm, head lower than body.
Deliver 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with heel of hand.
Flip infant face-up on your other forearm. Give 5 chest thrusts.
Alternate back blows and chest thrusts until object is expelled or infant becomes unconscious.

name: Dental Abscess Field Management
steps: Recognize: throbbing toothache, swelling of gum or face, fever, bad taste in mouth.
Rinse with warm salt water (½ tsp salt in 8oz water) every 2 hours.
Pain management: ibuprofen 400-600mg every 6 hours.
If swelling spreads to eye, neck, or floor of mouth: EMERGENCY. This can become life-threatening.

name: Improvised Temporary Filling
steps: Clean the cavity: rinse mouth with warm salt water, remove any debris.
Dry the tooth as much as possible.
Fill with temporary filling material, sugar-free gum, or candle wax.
Avoid chewing on that side. Replace the improvised filling daily.

name: Jaw Dislocation Reduction
steps: Recognize: jaw locked open, patient cannot close mouth, drooling, extreme pain.
Wrap your thumbs in thick cloth for protection.
Place wrapped thumbs on the lower molars. Apply steady DOWNWARD then BACKWARD pressure.
The jaw should click back into place. Apply a bandage wrap to limit opening for 24 hours.

name: Postpartum Hemorrhage Management
steps: Recognize: soaking more than one pad per hour, large clots, dizziness, rapid pulse.
Fundal massage: place hand on lower abdomen and massage the uterus firmly.
Encourage breastfeeding immediately — nipple stimulation causes uterine contraction.
If bleeding won't stop: bimanual compression — one fist inside the vagina against the uterus, other hand on abdomen.

name: Emergency Tourniquet (High and Tight)
steps: Place tourniquet as HIGH and TIGHT on the limb as possible.
Tighten until ALL bleeding stops and distal pulse is absent.
Note the TIME. Write 'TQ' and time on the patient's forehead.
A tourniquet can safely stay on for 6+ hours in most cases.

name: Flail Chest Stabilization
steps: Recognize: section of chest wall moves INWARD during inhalation (paradoxical motion).
Position patient with the INJURED SIDE DOWN.
Do NOT tape or strap the chest — this restricts breathing.
Support ventilation. If patient tires and breathing becomes shallow: assist ventilation with bag-valve-mask if available.

name: Anaphylaxis Emergency Protocol
steps: Recognize: rapid onset of hives/swelling + breathing difficulty or low blood pressure.
Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) immediately if available — outer thigh, through clothing.
Position: sitting up if breathing difficulty, lying down with legs elevated if low blood pressure.
Antihistamines (Benadryl) are SECOND-LINE, NOT a replacement for epinephrine.

name: Finger Dislocation Reduction
steps: Confirm dislocation: finger is obviously crooked at a joint, painful, unable to bend.
Grip the dislocated segment firmly. Pull steady traction ALONG the finger axis.
As traction is applied, gently guide the tip back into normal alignment.
Buddy-tape to adjacent finger for 2-3 weeks. Ice and ibuprofen for swelling.

name: Hemostatic Agent Application
steps: Pack the wound channel DEEP with hemostatic gauze.
Continue packing until wound is completely filled.
Apply direct pressure over the packed wound for 3+ minutes.
Cover with standard bandage, pressure bandage, or elastic wrap.

name: Improvised Chest Seal from Plastic
steps: Find any non-porous material: credit card, plastic bag, duct tape, foil wrapper.
Wipe blood from skin around wound to help the seal stick.
Apply the material over the wound. Tape on THREE sides only.
Monitor for tension pneumothorax: worsening breathing, distended neck veins.

name: Traumatic Amputation Field Care
steps: Control bleeding from the stump: tourniquet high and tight, or direct pressure + packing.
Recover the amputated part if safe to do so.
Wrap the amputated part in moist gauze, seal in a plastic bag.
Transport patient and part together to the nearest surgical facility.

name: Seizure Management (Field)
steps: DO NOT restrain the patient or put anything in their mouth.
Clear the area of hard/sharp objects. Cushion the head.
Time the seizure. Turn on side (recovery position) after convulsions stop.
Stay with patient during the postictal (recovery) phase — they'll be confused and sleepy.

name: Heat Stroke Emergency Cooling
steps: Recognize: hot DRY skin (sweating has stopped), altered mental status, core temp >104°F.
COOL BY ANY MEANS NECESSARY: immerse in cold water, pack ice in armpits/groin/neck.
If no cold water: spray/pour water on skin and fan aggressively.
Stop cooling when core temp reaches 102°F to avoid overshoot hypothermia.

name: Toothache Emergency Relief
steps: Clove oil (eugenol) is the strongest natural dental anesthetic — apply directly to cavity or gum.
Oil of oregano is a backup — strong antibacterial and mild anesthetic.
Ibuprofen 400mg + acetaminophen 500mg together (this combination exceeds either alone — proven in studies).
Warm salt water rinse every 2 hours.

name: Mild Hypothermia Self-Treatment
steps: Recognize in yourself: uncontrollable shivering, fumbling hands, stumbling, confused thinking.
Get out of wind and wet — shelter is priority one.
Create insulation between yourself and the ground.
Exercise vigorously if you can — generate metabolic heat.

name: Anaphylaxis: Epinephrine Administration
steps: Recognize anaphylaxis.
Prepare the Epinephrine Auto-Injector (EpiPen).
Inject into the outer mid-thigh.
Massage the area and prepare for a second dose.

name: Combat Application Tourniquet (CAT)
steps: Identify massive arterial bleeding.
Apply the CAT 'High and Tight'.
Tighten the windlass until bleeding stops.
Secure the windlass and mark the time.

name: Open Fracture Management
steps: Control bleeding first.
Irrigate heavily if evacuation is delayed (>6 hours).
Dress and splint in place.
Do NOT attempt to push the bone back in.

name: Styptic: Yarrow Poultice
steps: Identify Yarrow.
Chew or crush the fresh leaves.
Pack directly into the wound.
Utilize the alkaloid properties.

name: Frostbite: Thaw vs. Wait
steps: Assess the risk of re-freezing.
Determine evacuation capability.
Shelter-in-place scenario.
Protect thawed tissue.

name: Differentiating Heat Exhaustion vs Stroke
steps: Check the mental status.
Check the skin condition.
Treat Exhaustion with rest and hydration.
Treat Stroke with immediate, utterly aggressive cooling.

name: Pit Viper Envenomation Protocol
steps: Do no further harm.
Keep the patient calm and immobile.
Splint the bitten extremity.
Mark swelling progression with a pen.

name: Tripod Cooking Setup
steps: Lash three sturdy poles together at the top using a tripod lashing.
Spread the legs and set the tripod over your fire.
Adjust cooking temperature by raising or lowering the pot.
The tripod is the most stable camp cooking system.
Remove the pot using a sturdy stick through the bail handle.

name: Fireweed as Food & Tea
steps: Identify: tall pink flower spikes on cleared or burned land.
Young shoots: eat like asparagus (steam or boil 3 min).
Mature leaves: dry for tea — this is traditional Russian Ivan Chai.
Stem pith: sweet and starchy, eat raw.

name: Battery & Gum Wrapper Fire
steps: Cut a gum wrapper into an hourglass shape.
Touch the foil ends to the positive and negative terminals of a battery.
Transfer the flame to your tinder bundle immediately.

name: Steel Wool & 9V Battery
steps: Fluff up a piece of fine steel wool (Grade 0000 is best).
Touch the 9V battery terminals to the steel wool.
Blow on the glowing wool and add tinder.

name: Duct Tape Butterfly Stitch
steps: Cut two small strips of duct tape. Twist the middle of one strip.
Stick one end to skin, pull wound closed, then stick other end.
Method 2: Cut 'H' or Butterfly shape from tape.

name: Hazmat Evacuation
steps: Determine wind direction.
Move CROSSWIND (perpendicular to wind).
Seek higher ground.

name: Nuclear Fallout Protection
steps: Get inside immediately. Deep is good.
Stay inside for at least 24-72 hours.
Seal vents and windows.

name: Vehicular Mob Evasion
steps: Keep all doors locked and windows rolled up.
Maintain slow, continuous forward momentum.
Do not turn off the engine unless immobilized or on fire.

name: Heat Exhaustion
steps: Move to a cooler area out of direct sunlight.
Remove excess clothing and apply cool water.
Drink cool water or sports drinks slowly.
Monitor for heat stroke signs.

name: Sunburn Treatment
steps: Get out of the sun immediately.
Cool the skin with a cool bath or damp cloth.
Apply aloe vera or moisturizing lotion.
Hydrate heavily.

name: Emergency Decontamination
steps: Brush off dry powder BEFORE applying water.
Remove contaminated clothing carefully.
Flush with lukewarm water for 15+ minutes.

name: Heat Stroke
steps: Call emergency services immediately.
Cool the person rapidly.
Do NOT give fluids if unconscious.

name: Severe Burn Treatment
steps: Stop the burning process.
Cover loosely with sterile, non-stick bandage.
Treat for shock.

name: Evasion & Escape
steps: Break line of sight immediately.
Change your appearance/silhouette.
Move against the flow or into complexity.

name: Gray Man Theory
steps: Dress to match the average baseline of the crowd.
Mask your movement and energy.
Remove identifying markers.

name: Antisurveillance
steps: Use shop windows as mirrors.
Make a logical turn or stop.
Do not react if confirmed.

name: Seal a Room (Shelter in Place)
steps: Choose an interior room with few windows.
Turn off all ventilation (HVAC, fans).
Tape plastic sheeting over windows, doors, and vents.

name: GPS Battery Conservation
sourceName: FM 3-25.26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
steps: Turn off GPS between fixes
Use airplane mode on phones
Lower screen brightness
Keep battery warm

name: Establishing Daily Routine
steps: Wake at same time daily. Begin with a task immediately.
Set specific goals for each day—even small ones.
Maintain hygiene even when it seems pointless.
End day reviewing accomplishments and planning tomorrow.

name: Establishing Normalcy & Routine
steps: Establish a daily schedule within the first 24 hours.
Assign roles and responsibilities to every group member.
Maintain personal hygiene and camp organization.

name: Immediate Grief Triage
steps: Accept the acute shock response.
Prevent irrational actions.
Focus on the living.
Assign them a mechanical, repetitive task.
Do not offer false platitudes.

name: The Survival Circle — Managing Group Dynamics
steps: At the start of any survival situation: gather everyone in a circle face-to-face.
Each person reports: name, any injuries, special skills, inventory of personal gear.
Establish three things: leader, immediate priorities, individual assignments.
Schedule regular circle check-ins: morning briefing, evening debrief.

name: Establish the Baseline
steps: Observe the environment passively.
Identify the anomaly.
Categorize the anomaly.
React preemptively.

name: Charcoal Toothpaste
steps: Crush charcoal from your fire into a fine powder.
Mix with a little water (and salt/mint if available).
Scrub teeth with finger or chew stick.

name: Maximizing Phone Battery in Emergency
steps: Turn on airplane mode immediately.
Turn off airplane mode ONLY to attempt calls/texts, then back on.
Send texts instead of calling — texts use far less power.
Emergency numbers (112, 911, 999, etc.) work on any network and often without a SIM.
Before battery dies: text your GPS coordinates to someone.

name: Tire Fire Smoke Signal
steps: Burning rubber (tires, shoe soles, rubber mats) produces thick, black smoke visible for miles.
Build a hot base fire first with dry wood. Once burning strongly, add rubber items.
If near a vehicle: use a spare tire, floor mats, or rubber hoses. Cut into pieces for controlled burning.
Alternate rubber (black smoke) with green vegetation (white smoke) for maximum contrast.
CAUTION: burning rubber is toxic. Stand upwind. Do NOT inhale the fumes.

name: Active Shooter Geometry
steps: Evacuate immediately (RUN).
Understand Cover vs. Concealment.
Slice the pie around corners.
Barricade deeply (HIDE).
Ambush with improvised weapons (FIGHT).

name: Riot & Mob Evasion
steps: Move perpendicularly.
Avoid choke points.
Keep your chest protected.
Stay on your feet.

name: Adult CPR Protocol
steps: Confirm unresponsive: tap shoulders, shout. Call for help.
Check for breathing: look, listen, feel for 10 seconds maximum.
30 compressions: center of chest, 2 inches deep, hard and fast (100-120/min).
2 rescue breaths: tilt head back, lift chin, seal mouth. Watch for chest rise.

name: Adult Choking — Heimlich Maneuver
steps: Confirm choking: ask 'Can you breathe?' If they can't speak, cough, or breathe — act.
Stand behind the patient. Fist above navel, below ribcage.
Deliver sharp INWARD and UPWARD thrusts.
Repeat until object is expelled or patient becomes unconscious.

name: H.E.L.P. & Huddle Positions
sourceName: US Coast Guard Rescue & Survival Systems Manual
steps: Understand the cold shock response.
Minimize movement.
Assume the H.E.L.P. position (Solo).
Form a Huddle (Group).

name: Taut-Line Hitch
steps: Loop rope around anchor (tree, stake).
Make 2 wraps inside the loop around the standing part.
Make 1 wrap outside the loop above the coils.
Slide the knot to adjust tension.

name: Throwing Sling
steps: Cut two 24-inch lengths of cordage and a leather or woven pouch.
Attach cords to each side of the pouch.
Load a round stone and swing overhead in a circular motion.
Release the knotted cord at the right moment to launch the projectile.

name: Bamboo Multi-Tool Construction
steps: Select a green bamboo section at least 1 inch diameter.
Split lengthwise by scoring and applying pressure. Shape the edge with a rock.
Fire-harden the edge by slowly rotating over hot coals. Do not burn.

name: Sling Weapon
steps: Cut two equal cords, each about 2.5 feet long. Any strong cordage works — paracord, braided plant fiber.
Create a pouch in the center from leather, woven cloth, or a flat piece of bark, 3x5 inches.
Attach each cord to opposite ends of the pouch. Tie a finger loop on one cord and leave the other plain.
Load a smooth, round stone (golf ball to tennis ball size) in the pouch.
Swing overhead 1-2 times, then release the plain cord at the point aimed at the target.

name: Deadfall Safety Techniques
steps: Always approach from the side
Use a safety stick
Test trigger sensitivity before loading weight
Mind your fingers

name: Anchor Hitch (Fisherman's Bend)
sourceName: Animated Knots by Grog / Ashley Book of Knots
steps: Pass the rope through the ring twice.
Bring the working end across and under both turns.
Make a half hitch around the standing part.
Add a second half hitch for security.

name: Spanish Bowline
sourceName: Animated Knots by Grog / Ashley Book of Knots
steps: Form two side-by-side loops in the rope.
Reach through each loop and grab the opposite side rope.
Pull both grabs through to form interlocking bights.
Adjust loop sizes as needed. Each loop can be sized independently.

name: Adjustable Grip Hitch
sourceName: Animated Knots by Grog / Ashley Book of Knots
steps: Wrap the rope around the anchor point and back toward the load.
Make two wraps around the standing part, moving toward the anchor.
Cross over and make one wrap on the load side.
Tuck the end and dress. Slide to adjust tension.

name: Girth Hitch (Lark's Foot)
sourceName: Animated Knots by Grog / Ashley Book of Knots
steps: Pass the sling/loop around or through the object.
Thread one end of the loop through the other end.
Pull tight — the sling chokes around the object.
To release: push the choking loop back through.

name: Scaffold Knot (Poacher's Knot)
sourceName: Animated Knots / Ashley Book of Knots
steps: Form a loop in the rope.
Wrap the working end around BOTH sides of the loop 3+ times.
Pass the working end through the remaining loop opening.
Pull the standing end — the noose tightens. Pull the working end to adjust.

name: Birch Tar Production (Natural Adhesive)
steps: Collect birch bark — fresh or recently fallen, paper-thin outer bark.
Double-pot method: place bark in a sealed container (clay pot, tin can) upside down over a collection pot.
Heat the top container — the tar drips down into the collection pot.
The resulting black tar is waterproof adhesive — used for hafting tools, sealing containers, patching boats.

name: Iodine Treatment
steps: Add 5 drops of 2% iodine tincture per quart of clear water.
Wait 30 minutes before drinking.
Add vitamin C powder after wait time to improve taste.

name: Charcoal Water Filter
steps: Make charcoal from hardwood burned down to coals.
Crush charcoal into fine granules.
Build a filter: container with hole → grass → charcoal → sand → gravel.
Run water through multiple times for clearer results.

name: Iodine Water Purification
steps: Add 5 drops of 2% iodine tincture per liter of clear water.
If using iodine tablets (like Potable Aqua), add 2 tablets per liter.
Wait 30 minutes before drinking.
The water will taste slightly of iodine.
Pregnant women and people with thyroid conditions should avoid iodine.

name: Purification: Chlorine Dioxide Drops
steps: Mix Parts A and B.
Add to 1 liter of clear water.
Wait the prescribed contact time.
Note the water temperature.
